annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: the area of the square whose side is the hypotenuse

annotations.note: Mathlib states the Pythagorean theorem as an iff in inner-product form (angle = π/2) for points in a Euclidean affine space.

annotations.label: Pythagorean equation

anchor.snippet: sometimes called the Pythagorean equation

annotations.note: The equation a²+b²=c² is captured by the definition `PythagoreanTriple x y z : x*x + y*y = z*z`.

anchors.snippet: sometimes called the Pythagorean equation

annotations.label: Rearrangement proof

anchor.snippet: In one rearrangement proof, two squares are used

annotations.note: Mathlib has the theorem but does not formalize this particular visual rearrangement proof.

annotations.label: Algebraic proof with four triangles around square of side c

anchor.snippet: proved algebraically using four copies of the same triangle arranged symmetrically around a square with side c

annotations.note: No formalization of this specific geometric dissection argument in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: proved algebraically using four copies of the same triangle arranged symmetrically around a square with side c

annotations.label: Algebraic proof with triangles inside square of side c

anchor.snippet: A similar proof uses four copies of a right triangle

annotations.note: No formalization of this specific dissection proof in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A similar proof uses four copies of a right triangle

annotations.label: Proof using similar triangles

anchor.snippet: based on the proportionality of the sides of three similar triangles

annotations.note: The Pythagorean theorem in Mathlib is proved via inner products, not via a similar-triangles argument.

annotations.label: Einstein's dissection proof

anchor.snippet: In one proof by dissection, sometimes attributed to Albert Einstein

annotations.note: No formalization of Einstein's dissection-by-similarity proof in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid's proof (Elements Book I, Proposition 47)

anchor.snippet: how the proof in Euclid 's Elements (Proposition 47 of Book I) proceeds

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a formalization of Euclid's synthetic proof.

annotations.label: Rearrangement proof with central square

anchor.snippet: Another by rearrangement is given by the middle animation

annotations.note: No formalization of this animated rearrangement proof in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dissection proof from images

anchor.snippet: The third, rightmost image also gives a proof

annotations.note: No formalization of this specific dissection proof in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proof by area-preserving shearing

anchor.snippet: area-preserving shear mappings and translations can transform the squares

annotations.note: No formalization of this shear-mapping proof in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Garfield's trapezoid proof

anchor.snippet: A related proof by U.S. president James A. Garfield

annotations.note: No formalization of Garfield's trapezoid proof in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A related proof by U.S. president James A. Garfield

annotations.label: Proof using differentials

anchor.snippet: One can arrive at the Pythagorean theorem by studying how changes in a side produce a change in the hypotenuse

annotations.note: Mathlib does not derive Pythagoras from a differential ODE argument.

annotations.label: Converse of the Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: if the angle between sides

annotations.note: Mathlib's Pythagorean iff statement gives both directions, including the converse (a²+b²=c² implies right angle).

annotations.label: Existence of triangle from Pythagorean sides

anchor.snippet: there exists a triangle with sides

annotations.note: Mathlib doesn't have a dedicated existence-of-triangle-from-Pythagorean-sides construction.

annotations.label: Converse in Euclid's Elements Book I Proposition 48

anchor.snippet: This converse appears in Euclid's Elements (Book I, Proposition 48)

annotations.note: Captured by the same iff statement; Euclid's synthetic proof is not formalized but the mathematical content matches.

annotations.label: Right/acute/obtuse triangle classification

anchor.snippet: a simple means of determining whether a triangle is right, obtuse, or acute

annotations.note: Mathlib has the law of cosines (`law_cos`) but no direct trichotomy lemma classifying a triangle by comparing c² to a²+b².

annotations.label: Dijkstra's formulation

anchor.snippet: Edsger W. Dijkstra has stated this proposition about acute, right, and obtuse triangles

annotations.note: Dijkstra's sign-based formulation is not present as a named lemma.

anchors.snippet: Edsger W. Dijkstra has stated this proposition about acute, right, and obtuse triangles

annotations.label: Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: A Pythagorean triple has three positive integers

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple x y z := x*x + y*y = z*z` is defined on integers (without the positivity restriction).

annotations.label: Primitive Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: A primitive Pythagorean triple is one in which

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the predicate `PythagoreanTriple x y z ∧ Int.gcd x y = 1` rather than introducing a separate definition `IsPrimitive`.

annotations.label: List of primitive Pythagorean triples under 100

anchor.snippet: a list of primitive Pythagorean triples with values less than 100

annotations.note: Mathlib does not include an enumerated list of primitive triples.

annotations.label: Euclid's formula for Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: Euclid's formula is the most well-known

annotations.note: The `classification` theorem characterizes all Pythagorean triples in terms of (k,m,n) parameters.

anchors.snippet: Euclid's formula is the most well-known

annotations.label: Inverse (reciprocal) Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: the inverse Pythagorean theorem relates the two legs

annotations.note: No formalization of the reciprocal identity 1/a² + 1/b² = 1/h² in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the inverse Pythagorean theorem relates the two legs

annotations.label: Construction of incommensurable lengths

anchor.snippet: line segments whose lengths are incommensurable

annotations.note: The irrationality of √2 (canonical incommensurable diagonal of a unit square) is formalized.

annotations.label: Modulus of a complex number

anchor.snippet: the absolute value or modulus is given by

annotations.note: Complex.normSq z = z.re^2 + z.im^2, and `Complex.norm_def` / `‖z‖` give the modulus √(re²+im²).

anchors.snippet: the absolute value or modulus is given by

annotations.label: Distance between complex numbers

anchor.snippet: This can be generalised to find the distance between two points

annotations.note: `Complex.dist_eq : dist z w = ‖z - w‖`, with the modulus formula above giving the Pythagorean-type expression.

anchors.snippet: This can be generalised to find the distance between two points

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in the plane

anchor.snippet: The distance formula in Cartesian coordinates is derived from the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: The 2D case is the n=2 instance of the n-dimensional EuclideanSpace.dist_eq formula.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in n-space

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean distance between two points

annotations.note: `dist x y = √(∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2)` on `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 n`.

annotations.label: Squared Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: the squared Euclidean distance , or SED

annotations.note: `dist x y ^ 2 = ∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2` is given directly.

annotations.label: Distance in polar coordinates / law of cosines

anchor.section: Euclidean distance in other coordinate systems
anchor.snippet: the polar coordinates ( r , θ ) can be introduced

annotations.note: `law_cos` is exactly the polar-coordinate distance formula c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos θ.

annotations.label: Pythagorean trigonometric identity

anchor.snippet: the fundamental Pythagorean trigonometric identity

annotations.note: `sin x ^ 2 + cos x ^ 2 = 1` is provided for both ℝ and ℂ.

anchors.snippet: the fundamental Pythagorean trigonometric identity

annotations.label: Pythagorean relation between cross product and dot product

anchor.snippet: The Pythagorean theorem relates the cross product and dot product

annotations.note: Mathlib has ‖a × b‖ = ‖a‖‖b‖ sin θ and `cos_angle_mul_norm_mul_norm` for the dot-product side; the identity ‖a‖²‖b‖² = ⟪a,b⟫² + ‖a×b‖² follows but isn't stated separately.

anchors.snippet: The Pythagorean theorem relates the cross product and dot product

annotations.label: Equivalence with parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: Euclid's fifth postulate implies the Pythagorean theorem and vice-versa

annotations.note: Mathlib does not develop axiomatic Euclidean geometry; the parallel-postulate equivalence is not stated.

annotations.label: Similar figures on the three sides

anchor.section: Similar figures on the three sides
anchor.snippet: If one erects similar figures

annotations.note: No formalization of the area-of-similar-figures generalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Law of cosines

anchor.snippet: a special case of the more general theorem relating the lengths of sides in any triangle

annotations.note: `law_cos` (aliasing `dist_sq_eq_dist_sq_add_dist_sq_sub_two_mul_dist_mul_dist_mul_cos_angle`) is the standard law of cosines.

anchors.snippet: a special case of the more general theorem relating the lengths of sides in any triangle

annotations.note: Thābit's generalization is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Pappus's area theorem is a further generalization

annotations.note: Pappus's area theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ptolemy's theorem

anchor.snippet: Ptolemy's theorem states that when a quadrilateral can be inscribed in a circle

annotations.note: Stated for four cospherical points; this is Freek's 100 theorems #95.

annotations.label: Cuboid diagonal via Pythagoras

anchor.snippet: The length of face diagonal AC is found from Pythagoras's theorem

annotations.note: Follows directly from EuclideanSpace.dist_sq_eq in ℝ³, but no specific cuboid-diagonal lemma is named.

annotations.label: de Gua's theorem

anchor.snippet: de Gua's theorem , named for Jean Paul de Gua de Malves

annotations.note: de Gua's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: n-dimensional Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: the square of the volume of the hypotenuse

annotations.note: The Conant-Beyer simplex-volume Pythagorean theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem in inner product spaces

anchor.snippet: In an inner-product space, the Pythagorean theorem states that for any two orthogonal vectors

annotations.note: `‖x + y‖² = ‖x‖² + ‖y‖²` when ⟪x,y⟫ = 0 in any inner product space.

anchors.snippet: In an inner-product space, the Pythagorean theorem states that for any two orthogonal vectors

annotations.label: Parallelogram law

anchor.snippet: the parallelogram law

annotations.note: `‖x + y‖² + ‖x - y‖² = 2(‖x‖² + ‖y‖²)` is `parallelogram_law_with_norm`.

anchors.snippet: the parallelogram law

annotations.label: Pythagorean identity for n orthogonal vectors

anchor.snippet: The Pythagorean identity can be extended to sums of more than two orthogonal vectors

annotations.note: `‖∑ V i (l i)‖² = ∑ ‖l i‖²` for an orthogonal family generalizes the n-vector case.

anchors.snippet: The Pythagorean identity can be extended to sums of more than two orthogonal vectors

annotations.label: Generalization to measurable sets

anchor.section: Sets of m -dimensional objects in n -dimensional space
anchor.snippet: Another generalization of the Pythagorean theorem applies to Lebesgue-measurable sets

annotations.note: The Conant-Beyer measure-theoretic generalization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Failure on the sphere octant

anchor.snippet: in spherical geometry , all three sides of the right triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal spherical-triangle development to host this counterexample.

annotations.label: Generalization with angle sum condition

anchor.snippet: the Pythagorean theorem remains true in hyperbolic geometry and elliptic geometry

annotations.note: No formal hyperbolic/elliptic right-triangle Pythagorean characterization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spherical Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: For any right triangle on a sphere of radius R

annotations.note: The spherical Pythagorean formula cos(c/R) = cos(a/R)cos(b/R) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemannian metric tensor

anchor.snippet: which is called the metric tensor

annotations.note: A Riemannian metric is formalized as an inner product on each fiber of a vector bundle via `RiemannianBundle` / `IsRiemannianManifold`.
